facade_colour: cream-white painted wood siding with brown porch posts, trim bands, and a dark brown gable edge

hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Row, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a row, the properties retain most of their original building features including cross-gabled rooflines, double- height bay windows, brick cladding (at 31 and 33 Nassau Street), decorative wooden gable brackets and pediments, and fenestration pattern with flat headed openings featuring stone lintels and sills. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: One-way EB | 40 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks | Mixed commercial-residential

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: cream-white painted wood siding with brown porch posts, trim bands, and a dark brown gable edge
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: The house appears occupied and maintained, with painted woodwork in fair to good condition and some minor weathering on the porch elements.
photo_observations.window_details: Second-floor windows are tall and narrow under the gable, while the ground floor includes a porch-enclosed entry bay and adjacent front window.
photo_observations.door_details: Front door is recessed beneath a full-width porch roof and partially screened by posts and lattice.
photo_observations.cornice_details: Painted trim and patterned gable ornament emphasize the Victorian bay-and-gable form.
photo_observations.bay_window_details: Shallow projecting bay form is integrated into the upper facade beneath the front gable.
photo_observations.porch_details: Full-width open porch with posts, low railings, and paved front yard behind an ornate metal fence.
photo_observations.chimney_details: No chimney clearly visible from the frontal street photo.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Applied gable ornament, narrow trim boards, and a decorative metal front fence are the main visible embellishments.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Replacement windows and porch repairs are visible, but the overall bay-and-gable composition remains intact.
photo_observations.notes: Centered daytime view of the white painted bay-and-gable house.

notes: Centered daytime view of the white painted bay-and-gable house.

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its house-form, row, bay-and-gable typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double hung, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Character-defining facade elements include bay window, original windows, cornice.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Constructed as a row, the properties retain most of their original building features including cross-gabled rooflines, double- height bay windows, brick cladding (at 31 and 33 Nassau Street), decorative wooden gable brackets and pediments, and fenestration pattern with flat headed openings featuring stone lintels and sills. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.

facade_description: Two-storey bay-and-gable row house, c. 1890-1903, clad in cream-white painted wood clapboard with brown trim and porch posts. Full-width open front porch with decorative gable ornament. Double-height bay window beneath the front gable. Party walls on both sides; 3 dwelling units. Good condition with replacement windows and porch repairs visible.
heritage_summary: Constructed as a row, the properties retain most of their original building features including cross-gabled rooflines, double- height bay windows, brick cladding (at 31 and 33 Nassau Street), decorative wooden gable brackets and pediments, and fenestration pattern with flat headed openings featuring stone lintels and sills. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.
